title: Generation of Quantum Turbulence by Neutrino Cooling in Neutron Stars
abstract: The interior crust and much of the liquid core of neutron stars is believed to be a quantum liquid mixture of neutron and proton superfluids and a relativistic electron liquid. Quantized vortices in the neutron superfluid and quantized flux lines in the proton superconductor are topological defects of these hadronic condensates. I consider the formation of the superfluid state in young neutron stars under non-equilibrium conditions imposed by the neutrino cooling rate. The nonequilibrium phase transition implies that the onset of superfluidity is accompanied by the generation of quantized vortices based on the mechanism envisioned by Kibble in the context cosmic string formation in an evolutionary models of an expanding universe, and further developed by Zurek for nonequilibrium phase transitions in quantum liquids such as \Hefour. I discuss the Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM) and scaling relations for topological defect formation starting from the Cooper pair fluctuation propagator for temperatures approaching $T_c$. I then calculate the predicted vortex densities based on Urca and modified Urca cooling mechanisms in the cores of neutron stars for several models of the superfluid gap and transition temperature of the interior neutron superfluid. In all cases studied the KZM leads to a large density of topological defects in the condensate phase, which in 3D form a random network of vortex lines and loops, i.e. the generation of quantum turbulence.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the rapid neutrino cooling in young neutron stars acts as a thermal quench that triggers the Kibble-Zurek mechanism. This process generates a high density of quantized vortices, resulting in a state of quantum turbulence within the star's superfluid core. These findings have significant implications for understanding pulsar rotation and thermal evolution.
main_problem: Investigating whether the rapid, non-equilibrium neutrino cooling in young neutron stars triggers the generation of quantum turbulence through the formation of topological defects.
main_result: The application of the Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM) predicts that rapid cooling leads to a very high density of quantized vortices and loops, creating a random network of quantum turbulence in the neutron star interior.
method: The author uses the Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM) and scaling relations derived from the Cooper pair fluctuation propagator to calculate vortex densities based on Urca and modified Urca cooling rates.
model_or_system: The system is the interior crust and liquid core of a young neutron star, modeled as a quantum liquid mixture of neutron and proton superfluids and a relativistic electron liquid undergoing a second-order phase transition.
key_observables: Areal density of phase vortices (nv), inter-vortex spacing (dv), and the quench rate (1/tau_Q).
important_parameters: Neutrino cooling timescales (tau_Q), transition temperatures (Tc), pairing gaps (Delta), and neutron density (kfn).
main_assumptions: The cooling is modeled as a temperature quench; the fraction of phase windings (f) is non-universal; the system is treated within the weak-coupling limit for pairing.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows neutron superfluid pairing calculations; Figure 2 shows a diagrammatic representation of the Cooper pair propagator; Figure 3 and 4 show the areal density of vortices as a function of neutron density for different cooling processes and nuclear potentials.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the neutron star interior system, applies the Kibble-Zurek mechanism to the non-equilibrium phase transition, derives scaling relations from the Cooper pair propagator, calculates vortex densities for specific cooling mechanisms (Urca/modified Urca), and compares results to standard pulsar rotation models.
why_interesting: The paper explores non-equilibrium phase transitions and the generation of topological defects via rapid quenches, which is highly relevant to the study of dynamics in many-body systems and the formation of defects in condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Neutron superfluid Gaps vs neutron Fermi wavenumber. Shown are calculated gaps for 1S0 pairing at densities above neutron drip up to kfn ≈1.5 fm−1 based on direct nuclear potentials reported in Ref. 22. The reduced 1S0 pairing gap is based on the one-loop renormalization group equations for interacting Fermi liquids from Ref. 23. At higher densities 3P2 superfluidity is predicted with some- what reduced pairing gaps in the inner core.24 N.B. The curves were generated from digitized data extracted from Fig. 2 of Ref. 22, Fig. 8 of Ref. 23 and Fig. 4 of Ref. 24.

FIG. 2. Cooper Pair Propagator. Γpp(p+Q, −p; p′ +Q, −p′) is the particle-particle irreducible vertex responsible for the binding of pairs of Fermions with nearly zero total four-momentum, Q ≪(pf, Ef).

FIG. 3. The aeral density of vortices generated by the KZM as a function of neutron Fermi wavenumber kfn, normalized by the areal density of equilibrium vortices for co-rotation at the Crab pulsar rotational velocity. The fraction f of ±2π phase windings at freeze out is varied between 10−3 −10−1. The quench rate through Tc is obtained from direct Urca neutrino cooling (top panel) and modified Urca neutrino cooling (bottom panel). The 3P2 gap and Tc are based on the Nijmegen-I potential from Ref. 24.
FIG. 4. Same caption as that in Fig. 3 except the 3P2 gap and Tc are based on the Argonne potential from Ref. 24.
